text: When a process creates a thread, does the new thread get its own copy of the process's heap memory, or does it share the same heap? Explain what is shared and what is private between threads of the same process.
follow_up_shallow: Can you specifically name which segments (stack, heap, code, data) are shared versus private for threads?
follow_up_wrong: If threads shared their stacks, what problem would occur when two threads call different functions simultaneously?
follow_up_strong: How does the Linux kernel internally represent threads versus processes — are they different data structures or the same?

text: Explain why a context switch between two threads of the same process is faster than a context switch between two threads belonging to different processes.
follow_up_shallow: What specific hardware registers and OS structures need to be saved and restored during a context switch?
follow_up_wrong: Does a thread-level context switch require flushing the TLB? Why or why not?
follow_up_strong: How do modern CPUs with tagged TLBs reduce the cost of process-level context switches, and what is an ASID?

text: In a Round Robin scheduling algorithm with a time quantum of 4ms, if three processes arrive at time 0 with burst times 5ms, 3ms, and 8ms respectively, what is the average waiting time? Walk through the Gantt chart.
follow_up_shallow: What happens to a process that finishes before its quantum expires — does the CPU sit idle for the remaining quantum?
follow_up_wrong: If you said process P2 waits for longer than P1, reconsider: P2 has a shorter burst time — how does that affect when it completes?
follow_up_strong: How would you choose an optimal time quantum for Round Robin, and what are the consequences of setting it too high or too low?

text: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling? Give one real-world scenario where non-preemptive scheduling would be preferred over preemptive.
follow_up_shallow: Can you name specific scheduling algorithms that fall into each category?
follow_up_wrong: You said FCFS is preemptive — reconsider whether a process running under FCFS can be interrupted by a newly arriving process.
follow_up_strong: In a real-time operating system, why might preemptive priority scheduling still miss deadlines, and how does Rate Monotonic Scheduling address this?

text: If a system has four processes and each process needs a maximum of 3 instances of a single resource type, what is the minimum number of resource instances needed to guarantee that deadlock will never occur?
follow_up_shallow: Can you explain the formula you used and why adding one extra instance beyond the worst case prevents deadlock?
follow_up_wrong: Consider the worst case: each process holds max-1 resources and needs one more. How many total resources are held in that scenario?
follow_up_strong: How does the Banker's Algorithm determine if a state is safe, and what is its time complexity with n processes and m resource types?

text: Explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock and describe which one is most commonly targeted by operating systems to prevent deadlock in practice.
follow_up_shallow: You mentioned mutual exclusion — can you give an example of a resource where mutual exclusion cannot be eliminated?
follow_up_wrong: You said 'no preemption' means processes cannot be killed — that's not quite right. What does the no-preemption condition actually mean in deadlock context?
follow_up_strong: Why is breaking circular wait via resource ordering practical, whereas breaking hold-and-wait often leads to starvation?

text: What is the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation? Which type of fragmentation does paging eliminate, and why does it not eliminate the other?
follow_up_shallow: Can you calculate the average internal fragmentation per page if the page size is 4KB?
follow_up_wrong: You said paging eliminates internal fragmentation — actually, the last page of a process may not be fully used. Which fragmentation does that cause?
follow_up_strong: How does the buddy system allocator trade off between internal fragmentation and allocation speed, and how does slab allocation improve upon it?

text: In a virtual memory system, when a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory, describe the exact sequence of events that occurs from the moment the CPU generates the address to when the process resumes execution.
follow_up_shallow: At which step in the page fault handling process does the OS decide whether to use a page replacement algorithm?
follow_up_wrong: You said the process continues immediately after the disk read is initiated — what actually happens to the process while the page is being loaded from disk?
follow_up_strong: How does demand paging differ from anticipatory paging, and what heuristics does the OS use to prefetch pages?

text: A system uses LRU page replacement with 3 page frames. For the reference string 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, what is the total number of page faults? Trace through each step.
follow_up_shallow: How does LRU determine which page was 'least recently used' — what data structure would you use to implement it efficiently?
follow_up_wrong: You replaced the wrong page at step 4 — remember, LRU evicts the page that was accessed the longest time ago, not the one that arrived first.
follow_up_strong: Why is true LRU expensive to implement in hardware, and how do real operating systems approximate LRU using the clock algorithm?

text: Explain the difference between a binary semaphore and a mutex. Can a thread that did not lock a mutex unlock it? What about a binary semaphore?
follow_up_shallow: What is the concept of 'ownership' in a mutex, and why does it matter for preventing priority inversion?
follow_up_wrong: You said a binary semaphore and a mutex are the same thing — consider what happens when a different thread tries to release them.
follow_up_strong: Explain priority inversion and how priority inheritance protocols solve it using mutex ownership semantics.

text: Compare shared memory and message passing as IPC mechanisms. In what scenario would you choose message passing over shared memory despite its higher overhead?
follow_up_shallow: What synchronization mechanisms are needed when two processes communicate via shared memory, and why?
follow_up_wrong: You said shared memory is always faster — consider the cost of synchronization and cache coherence in multi-core systems. Is it always faster?
follow_up_strong: How does the microkernel architecture's reliance on message passing affect performance, and what optimizations like LRPC mitigate this?

text: Explain how the inode-based file system structure works in ext4. What information does an inode store, and how does the system handle files larger than what direct block pointers can address?
follow_up_shallow: How many levels of indirect block pointers does ext4 support, and roughly how large a file can each level address?
follow_up_wrong: You said the inode stores the file name — actually, where is the file name stored in a Unix file system?
follow_up_strong: How do ext4 extents improve upon the traditional indirect block pointer scheme for handling large files?

text: What is the difference between a system call and a library function call? Describe the mode switch that occurs when a user-space program invokes the read() system call.
follow_up_shallow: What role does the interrupt vector table play during a system call?
follow_up_wrong: You said system calls don't involve a mode switch — reconsider: where does the kernel code execute, and can user-mode code access kernel memory?
follow_up_strong: How do modern CPUs use SYSCALL/SYSENTER instructions instead of software interrupts to reduce the overhead of system calls?

text: Define thrashing and explain the relationship between the degree of multiprogramming and CPU utilization. At what point does increasing multiprogramming actually decrease throughput?
follow_up_shallow: How does the working set model help detect and prevent thrashing?
follow_up_wrong: You said thrashing is caused by too much CPU usage — actually, what is the real bottleneck during thrashing?
follow_up_strong: How does Linux's OOM killer handle thrashing scenarios, and what heuristics does it use to decide which process to terminate?

text: In Shortest Job First scheduling, how does the OS know the burst time of a process before it has executed? Explain the prediction technique used and its limitations.
follow_up_shallow: What is the exponential averaging formula used for burst time prediction, and what does the alpha parameter control?
follow_up_wrong: You said SJF uses the actual burst time — in practice, the OS does not know future burst times. How is this handled?
follow_up_strong: Prove that SJF gives the minimum average waiting time for a given set of processes. Under what assumptions does this proof hold?

text: Explain the difference between logical address and physical address in a paging system. If the logical address space is 32-bit and the page size is 4KB, how many entries does the page table need?
follow_up_shallow: How is a logical address split into page number and offset, and what determines the number of bits for each?
follow_up_wrong: You calculated the page table size incorrectly — remember, 4KB = 2^12 bytes, and 32-bit address means 2^32 addresses. How many pages is that?
follow_up_strong: Why is a flat page table impractical for 64-bit address spaces, and how do multi-level page tables solve this problem?

text: What is Belady's anomaly? Which page replacement algorithms are susceptible to it, and which class of algorithms is provably immune? Explain why.
follow_up_shallow: Can you give a specific reference string where FIFO with 4 frames produces more page faults than with 3 frames?
follow_up_wrong: You said LRU suffers from Belady's anomaly — actually, LRU belongs to a class of algorithms immune to it. What class is that?
follow_up_strong: What is the stack property of page replacement algorithms, and how does it formally prove immunity to Belady's anomaly?

text: Explain the Producer-Consumer problem and implement a solution using semaphores. What happens if the order of wait operations on the semaphores is swapped?
follow_up_shallow: What are the three semaphores typically used, and what does each one protect?
follow_up_wrong: You put the mutex wait before the empty/full wait — explain the specific deadlock scenario this can cause.
follow_up_strong: How would you extend this solution to support multiple producers and multiple consumers with bounded buffer using lock-free techniques?

text: Explain the concept of copy-on-write in the context of the fork() system call. Why does this optimization matter, and what is the sequence of events when a child process modifies a shared page?
follow_up_shallow: How does the OS mark pages as copy-on-write at the hardware level?
follow_up_wrong: You said fork() copies all memory immediately — that would be extremely expensive. What actually happens with modern fork implementations?
follow_up_strong: Why is fork() followed by exec() still common despite vfork() being available? What are the safety trade-offs?

text: Compare segmentation with paging. Why did modern operating systems largely abandon pure segmentation in favor of paging, and what remnants of segmentation still exist in x86-64 architecture?
follow_up_shallow: What is the key advantage of segmentation over paging from the programmer's perspective?
follow_up_wrong: You said x86-64 uses segmentation fully — actually, long mode flattens most segment registers. Which registers still serve a purpose?
follow_up_strong: How does Intel's Memory Protection Keys (MPK) mechanism provide some of segmentation's protection benefits within a paged memory model?

text: A real-time operating system must schedule both hard real-time and soft real-time tasks on the same CPU. Design a scheduling strategy that ensures hard real-time deadlines are never missed while maximizing throughput for soft real-time tasks. Discuss the trade-offs of your approach.
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between Rate Monotonic and Earliest Deadline First scheduling?
follow_up_wrong: You said you would use Round Robin for hard real-time tasks — why is this problematic for deadline guarantees?
follow_up_strong: How does the schedulability test for Rate Monotonic Scheduling work, and what is the utilization bound for n tasks?

text: You are designing an operating system for a machine with only 512MB of physical RAM that needs to run applications with a combined virtual address space of 8GB. Describe your complete virtual memory strategy, including how you would handle the inevitable high rate of page faults and prevent thrashing.
follow_up_shallow: How would you size the swap partition, and what file system considerations affect swap performance?
follow_up_wrong: You said you would just add more RAM — assume the hardware constraint is fixed. What software strategies can you employ?
follow_up_strong: How does Linux's zswap or zram compress pages in memory before writing to swap, and what are the CPU vs I/O trade-offs?

text: Explain the Readers-Writers problem with writer priority. Why can writer priority lead to reader starvation, and design a fair solution that bounds the maximum wait time for both readers and writers without significantly degrading throughput.
follow_up_shallow: How does Reader priority differ from Writer priority in terms of which side can starve?
follow_up_wrong: Your solution still allows unbounded waiting for readers — how would adding a turnstile semaphore fix this?
follow_up_strong: How do read-write locks in modern languages like Java's ReentrantReadWriteLock implement fairness, and what does 'fair' versus 'non-fair' mode mean?

text: Explain the difference between a microkernel and a monolithic kernel in terms of which OS services run in kernel space. If you were designing an OS for a safety-critical medical device, which would you choose and why? Discuss the performance and reliability trade-offs.
follow_up_shallow: Name specific operating systems that use each architecture.
follow_up_wrong: You said microkernels are always slower — can you name scenarios where the reduced kernel-space code actually leads to better performance through reduced crash recovery time?
follow_up_strong: How does the seL4 microkernel achieve formally verified correctness, and what does this mean for safety-critical systems?

text: In a multi-processor system, a spinlock is sometimes preferred over a mutex for protecting very short critical sections. Explain why spinning can be more efficient than blocking in this scenario, and at what point does a spinlock become wasteful. How do hybrid locks like Linux's MCS locks improve upon naive spinlocks?
follow_up_shallow: What happens if a thread holding a spinlock gets preempted? Why is this a problem?
follow_up_wrong: You said spinlocks always waste CPU — why is the context switch cost of a mutex sometimes worse than spinning for short critical sections?
follow_up_strong: Explain the cache coherence traffic problem with test-and-set spinlocks and how test-and-test-and-set or MCS queue locks reduce bus contention.

text: Explain the difference between the ACID properties Consistency and Isolation. Give a concrete example of a database state that violates consistency but not isolation, and one that violates isolation but not consistency.
follow_up_shallow: Who is responsible for ensuring consistency — the DBMS or the application developer?
follow_up_wrong: You're conflating isolation with consistency. Isolation is about concurrent transactions, consistency is about integrity constraints. Can you try again?
follow_up_strong: How does the CAP theorem redefine 'consistency' compared to ACID's definition, and why does this cause confusion in distributed database discussions?

text: Given a table Student(StudentID, Name, CourseID, CourseName, InstructorID, InstructorName), identify all functional dependencies and normalize it to Third Normal Form. Show each step and explain what anomalies each normalization step eliminates.
follow_up_shallow: What specific insertion, update, or deletion anomaly exists in the original table?
follow_up_wrong: You decomposed the table but lost a functional dependency. How do you ensure lossless join and dependency preservation during normalization?
follow_up_strong: Explain the difference between 3NF and BCNF with a concrete example where a table satisfies 3NF but violates BCNF, and discuss whether BCNF decomposition always preserves dependencies.

text: Explain the difference between a clustered index and a non-clustered index. If a table already has a clustered index on the primary key, what happens internally when you create a non-clustered index on another column?
follow_up_shallow: Can a table have more than one clustered index? Why or why not?
follow_up_wrong: You said a non-clustered index stores the actual data rows — actually, what does a non-clustered index leaf node contain?
follow_up_strong: In a covering index, the query can be answered entirely from the index without touching the base table. How does this affect query performance, and what are the trade-offs of making an index covering?

text: What is a dirty read? Give a concrete scenario with two bank account transactions where a dirty read leads to an incorrect account balance being displayed to a user.
follow_up_shallow: Which isolation level is the minimum required to prevent dirty reads?
follow_up_wrong: You described a non-repeatable read, not a dirty read. A dirty read involves reading uncommitted data. Can you revise your scenario?
follow_up_strong: Explain the difference between dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads, and map each to the minimum isolation level that prevents it.

text: Explain the difference between an INNER JOIN and a LEFT OUTER JOIN with a concrete example. If Table A has 5 rows and Table B has 3 rows with 2 matching rows, how many rows does each type of join produce?
follow_up_shallow: What values appear in the columns from Table B for non-matching rows in a LEFT JOIN?
follow_up_wrong: You said LEFT JOIN always returns the same number of rows as the left table — what happens when a single row in the left table matches multiple rows in the right table?
follow_up_strong: Compare the performance characteristics of nested loop join, hash join, and sort-merge join. When does the query optimizer choose each?

text: What is the purpose of the WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) protocol in ensuring the durability property of ACID? What would happen if the system crashed after modifying data in memory but before the log was written to disk?
follow_up_shallow: What information does a typical WAL log record contain?
follow_up_wrong: You said the data is written to disk before the log — that violates WAL. What is the correct order, and why?
follow_up_strong: How does PostgreSQL's WAL archiving enable point-in-time recovery, and what is the difference between WAL shipping and streaming replication?

text: Explain why adding an index on a column speeds up SELECT queries but can slow down INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. Quantify the trade-off with an example.
follow_up_shallow: If a table has 5 indexes and you insert a new row, how many additional write operations occur?

follow_up_strong: How do databases like PostgreSQL handle the write amplification problem of indexes, and what is HOT (Heap Only Tuple) update optimization?

text: What is a self-join? Write a SQL query to find all employees who earn more than their manager, assuming an Employee table with columns EmployeeID, Name, Salary, and ManagerID.
follow_up_shallow: What table aliases are you using, and why are they necessary in a self-join?

follow_up_strong: How would you extend this query to find the employee who earns the most relative to their manager across all departments, handling NULLs for top-level managers?

text: Explain the difference between a stored procedure and a trigger. In what scenario would a trigger be a better choice than a stored procedure, and what are the risks of overusing triggers?
follow_up_shallow: Can a trigger call a stored procedure? Can a stored procedure fire a trigger?

follow_up_strong: Discuss the concept of cascading triggers and why they can lead to infinite loops. How do different databases handle this?

text: Explain the CAP theorem and why a distributed database cannot simultaneously guarantee all three properties. Give a specific example of a real-world database and which two properties it prioritizes.
follow_up_shallow: Where does MongoDB fall in the CAP triangle, and has its position changed across versions?
follow_up_wrong: You said a single-node database also faces CAP trade-offs — CAP applies specifically to distributed systems. Why?
follow_up_strong: How does Google's Spanner claim to offer strong consistency and high availability simultaneously using TrueTime, and does this violate CAP?

text: Explain how MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) allows readers to never block writers and writers to never block readers. Walk through a specific example with two concurrent transactions.
follow_up_shallow: How does the database decide which version of a row a transaction should see?
follow_up_wrong: You said MVCC prevents all anomalies — actually, what anomaly can still occur under snapshot isolation that does not occur under serializable?
follow_up_strong: Explain the write skew anomaly under snapshot isolation and how Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) in PostgreSQL detects and prevents it.

text: Compare B-tree and hash indexes. A developer wants to add an index to optimize a query that uses both equality checks and range scans on the same column. Which index type should they choose and why?
follow_up_shallow: Why can't a hash index support range queries efficiently?
follow_up_wrong: You recommended a hash index for range queries — hash indexes only support equality lookups. Why?
follow_up_strong: How does a B+ tree differ from a B-tree, and why do most databases use B+ trees for their on-disk indexes?

text: Explain how query execution plans work. If you run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a query and see a sequential scan on a table with an index on the scanned column, what could cause the optimizer to ignore the index?
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between a sequential scan, an index scan, and an index-only scan?
follow_up_wrong: You said the optimizer always uses an index if one exists — that's incorrect. What factor related to table size makes a sequential scan cheaper?
follow_up_strong: How do databases collect and update statistics for the query optimizer, and what happens to query performance when statistics become stale?

text: What is the difference between horizontal sharding and vertical sharding? A social media application has 100 million users and their posts. Design a sharding strategy and explain how you would handle queries that need to join data across shards.
follow_up_shallow: What is a shard key, and what makes a good shard key versus a bad one?
follow_up_wrong: You suggested sharding by user's first name letter — this creates hotspots because name distribution is uneven. What's a better approach?
follow_up_strong: How does consistent hashing help when you need to add or remove shards, and how does it compare to range-based sharding for write-heavy workloads?

text: Explain the difference between synchronous and asynchronous replication in databases. In a master-slave replication setup, what consistency guarantees does each approach provide, and what happens to data if the master fails before replication completes?
follow_up_shallow: What is replication lag, and how does it affect read-after-write consistency?
follow_up_wrong: You said asynchronous replication guarantees no data loss — what happens to writes that were committed on the master but not yet replicated?
follow_up_strong: How does semi-synchronous replication in MySQL balance data safety and performance, and how does it differ from group replication?

text: Compare SQL and NoSQL databases for a high-traffic e-commerce application that needs to handle both structured product catalog data and semi-structured user activity logs. Would you use one database or two? Justify your choice.

follow_up_shallow: What is a wait-for graph, and how does it help detect deadlocks?

follow_up_strong: Compare wound-wait and wait-die schemes for deadlock prevention in distributed database systems. Which is more appropriate for long-running transactions?

text: What are the four isolation levels defined by the SQL standard? For each level, name the specific concurrency anomaly it allows that the next higher level prevents.

follow_up_strong: Why does PostgreSQL's Repeatable Read implementation actually prevent phantom reads even though the SQL standard says it doesn't have to?

text: An e-commerce company currently uses a single PostgreSQL database handling 50,000 transactions per second, and they are hitting write throughput limits. For each option, discuss the complexity, data consistency implications, and when it would be the right choice.
follow_up_shallow: At what point would you consider sharding versus just adding more read replicas?

follow_up_strong: How does CockroachDB's use of the Raft consensus protocol enable serializable distributed transactions, and what latency trade-offs does this introduce?

text: Design the database schema and indexing strategy for a real-time analytics dashboard that needs to handle 10 million events per day with sub-second query response times. Events have a timestamp, user_id, event_type, and a JSON payload. The dashboard needs to support time-range queries, top-N event types, and per-user activity timelines. Justify your choice between a traditional RDBMS, a columnar database, and a time-series database.
follow_up_shallow: How would you partition the events table, and what would your partition key be?

follow_up_strong: How do columnar storage formats like Parquet and databases like ClickHouse achieve orders of magnitude better compression and query performance for analytics compared to row-oriented storage?

text: You are running a banking application on a distributed database with two data centers. A network partition occurs between the data centers while both are actively processing transactions. Explain exactly what happens under each CAP configuration (CP vs AP), how the system should behave for debit transactions versus balance inquiry transactions, and how you would implement automatic reconciliation when the partition heals.
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between strong consistency and eventual consistency in this scenario?

follow_up_strong: How does CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) handle the reconciliation problem, and why are they suitable for some data types but not others like account balances?

text: Explain the trade-offs between pessimistic locking (two-phase locking) and optimistic concurrency control. In a web application where users frequently edit shared documents, which approach would you choose? follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between shared locks and exclusive locks in two-phase locking?
follow_up_wrong: You said optimistic locking never causes aborts — what happens when two transactions modify the same data concurrently under OCC?
follow_up_strong: How does Google's Percolator implement distributed transactions using optimistic concurrency control with a global timestamp oracle, and what throughput trade-offs does this introduce?

text: A locking-based approach to implementing Serializable isolation is known to have the 'phantom problem'. Explain what phantom reads are with a concrete example, explain why standard row-level locks fail to prevent them, and describe how predicate locking or gap locking solves the problem.

follow_up_strong: How does MySQL InnoDB implement next-key locking to prevent phantoms, and what are the performance implications compared to PostgreSQL's SSI approach?

text: Explain why the OSI model has 7 layers while the TCP/IP model only has 4 layers. Which OSI layers are combined in the TCP/IP model, and for a web browser loading a webpage, identify which layer handles each task: HTML rendering, establishing a reliable connection, and routing the packet to the server.

follow_up_strong: Why do network engineers still use the OSI model for troubleshooting even though the internet runs on TCP/IP? Give a practical debugging scenario.

text: Explain the key differences between TCP and UDP. A video conferencing application uses UDP for audio/video but TCP for chat messages within the same application. Explain why each protocol was chosen for its respective task.

text: Walk through the complete TCP three-way handshake process. What specific information is exchanged in each of the three packets (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), and why is this process necessary before data transfer can begin?

follow_up_shallow: What is the role of the Certificate Authority in HTTPS, and what happens if the certificate is expired?

text: Describe the step-by-step process that occurs when you type 'www.google.com' in your browser's address bar and press Enter, focusing on DNS resolution. What happens at each level of the DNS hierarchy if the answer is not cached?

text: A company is given the IP address block 192.168.10.0/24 and needs to create 4 subnets, each accommodating at least 50 hosts. Calculate the subnet mask, the usable IP range for each subnet, and the broadcast address for each subnet.
follow_up_shallow: Why are two addresses in each subnet unusable for hosts? What are they reserved for?

follow_up_strong: Explain VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) and how it helps when subnets require different numbers of hosts, compared to fixed-size subnetting.

text: Explain what NAT (Network Address Translation) does and why it was created. If a home router uses NAT and three devices behind it access the same website simultaneously, how does the router know which response to forward to which device?
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between static NAT, dynamic NAT, and PAT (Port Address Translation)?
follow_up_wrong: You said each device gets a different public IP — with PAT, all devices share the same public IP. So how are they differentiated?
follow_up_strong: Why does NAT break end-to-end connectivity, and how do protocols like STUN, TURN, and ICE (used in WebRTC) work around NAT to enable peer-to-peer connections?

text: What is the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) and why is it necessary? If Host A wants to send a packet to Host B on the same local network, describe the exact sequence of events ARP performs to resolve Host B's MAC address.
follow_up_shallow: Why does ARP use a broadcast frame for the request but a unicast frame for the reply?
follow_up_wrong: You said ARP resolves IP addresses from MAC addresses — it's the other way around. What does ARP actually do?
follow_up_strong: What is ARP spoofing/poisoning, how can an attacker use it for a man-in-the-middle attack, and what defenses exist (e.g., Dynamic ARP Inspection)?

text: Compare IPv4 and IPv6. Beyond the larger address space, name three specific technical improvements that IPv6 introduced over IPv4 and explain why each improvement matters.
follow_up_shallow: How does IPv6 eliminate the need for NAT, and is that always considered an advantage?
follow_up_wrong: You said IPv6 is faster because it has a larger address — address size doesn't affect speed. What IPv6 header feature actually helps with performance?
follow_up_strong: Explain the dual-stack, tunneling, and translation approaches to IPv4-to-IPv6 transition, and why has IPv6 adoption been slower than expected?

text: What is ICMP and what is it used for? Explain how the traceroute command uses ICMP to discover the path packets take to reach a destination, including the role of TTL.
follow_up_shallow: What ICMP message type does a router send when it decrements TTL to zero?
follow_up_wrong: You said traceroute uses TCP — the traditional Unix traceroute uses UDP with incrementing TTL and ICMP error responses. Can you explain the mechanism?
follow_up_strong: Why do some network administrators block ICMP, what functionality breaks when they do, and what are the security concerns that justify blocking?

text: Describe the complete TLS 1.3 handshake process and explain how it achieves a secure connection in fewer round trips than TLS 1.2. What is the role of the Diffie-Hellman key exchange in TLS 1.3, and why was RSA key exchange removed?
follow_up_shallow: How many round trips does TLS 1.3 require compared to TLS 1.2 for a full handshake?
follow_up_wrong: You said TLS 1.3 still supports RSA key exchange — RSA key exchange was removed because it lacks forward secrecy. What does forward secrecy mean?
follow_up_strong: Explain 0-RTT resumption in TLS 1.3 and why it introduces replay attack vulnerabilities. How do servers mitigate this?

text: Compare WebSockets with HTTP long polling and Server-Sent Events (SSE). For a real-time stock ticker application displaying price updates every 100ms, which protocol would you choose and why?
follow_up_shallow: What is the WebSocket upgrade handshake, and how does it transition from HTTP to WebSocket?
follow_up_wrong: You said HTTP long polling is the same as WebSockets — they have fundamentally different connection models. What's the key difference?
follow_up_strong: How would you scale WebSocket connections across multiple server instances behind a load balancer, and what sticky session or pub/sub patterns are needed?

text: Explain how a CDN (Content Delivery Network) improves website performance. When a user in Tokyo accesses a website hosted in New York, describe the exact sequence of events that occurs with and without a CDN, and explain how the CDN knows what content to serve.
follow_up_shallow: How does the CDN handle content that is not in its cache — what is a cache miss?
follow_up_wrong: You said CDN stores all content permanently — CDN content has TTL values and can be invalidated. How does cache invalidation work?
follow_up_strong: Compare push CDN vs pull CDN architectures and explain how edge computing extends the CDN concept beyond static content caching.

text: Compare Layer 4 (transport-level) and Layer 7 (application-level) load balancers. For a microservices architecture where some services require routing based on HTTP headers and URL paths, which type would you deploy and at what points in your infrastructure?
follow_up_shallow: What load balancing algorithms can each type use — for example, can an L4 load balancer do cookie-based sticky sessions?
follow_up_wrong: You said L4 load balancers can inspect HTTP headers — L4 operates at the TCP level. What information does it have to make routing decisions?
follow_up_strong: How does a service mesh (like Istio or Envoy) handling L7 load balancing differ from a traditional reverse proxy, and what observability benefits does it provide?

text: Explain the difference between OSPF and BGP routing protocols. Why is BGP called a 'path vector' protocol while OSPF is a 'link state' protocol? In the internet's routing architecture, where is each one used?
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between an Interior Gateway Protocol and an Exterior Gateway Protocol?
follow_up_wrong: You said BGP is used within a single organization's network — BGP is the inter-AS protocol. Where within a network would OSPF be used?
follow_up_strong: How can BGP route hijacking occur, and what mechanisms like RPKI help prevent it?

text: Compare REST and gRPC for inter-service communication in a microservices architecture. Under what specific conditions would you choose gRPC over REST, and what challenges does gRPC introduce for browser-based clients?
follow_up_shallow: What serialization format does gRPC use, and why is it faster than JSON used by REST?
follow_up_wrong: You said gRPC works natively in browsers just like REST — actually, gRPC requires HTTP/2 framing that browsers don't expose directly. What is grpc-web?
follow_up_strong: How does gRPC's bidirectional streaming compare to GraphQL subscriptions for real-time data, and when would you choose each?

text: Explain the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy. In a corporate environment, how are each used, and what is the security role of each? Describe a specific architecture where both are used simultaneously.
follow_up_shallow: Can you name specific software that acts as a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx, HAProxy)?
follow_up_wrong: You described a forward proxy as sitting in front of the server — a forward proxy sits in front of the client. What is the correct position of each?
follow_up_strong: How does a transparent proxy intercept traffic without client configuration, and what are the privacy and security implications?

text: Explain TCP congestion control mechanisms. Walk through the Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery phases. What triggers the transition between each phase?
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between the congestion window (cwnd) and the receiver window (rwnd)?
follow_up_wrong: You said the congestion window resets to zero after packet loss — in Fast Recovery, it resets to half. When does it actually reset to 1 MSS?
follow_up_strong: How does Google's BBR congestion control algorithm differ fundamentally from traditional loss-based algorithms like Cubic and Reno?

text: You are tasked with designing the network architecture for a globally distributed application that must serve users on six continents with less than 100ms latency for API requests. Design the complete network stack including DNS strategy, CDN placement, load balancing tiers, and edge computing locations. Discuss how you would handle failover when an entire region goes down.
follow_up_shallow: How does GeoDNS help route users to the nearest datacenter?
follow_up_wrong: You ignored the speed of light constraint — if the server is 5000km away, what is the minimum round-trip time, and how does this limit your architecture?
follow_up_strong: How does Netflix's Open Connect achieve low-latency video delivery by embedding custom CDN appliances inside ISP networks?

text: A company's web application is experiencing intermittent connection timeouts that happen only during peak traffic. Users report that some requests take over 30 seconds while others complete normally. Using your knowledge of TCP, DNS, load balancing, and HTTP, describe your debugging approach layer by layer. What would you check at each layer, what tools would you use, and what are the three most likely root causes?
follow_up_shallow: What network diagnostic tools would you use to check for TCP retransmissions?
follow_up_wrong: You jumped straight to application-level debugging — network issues often manifest as application timeouts. Why should you check lower layers first?
follow_up_strong: How does eBPF-based network observability (like what Cilium provides) enable low-overhead packet tracing in production compared to traditional tcpdump?

text: Explain how a DDoS attack works at the network level. Compare volumetric attacks (UDP flood), protocol attacks (SYN flood), and application-layer attacks (HTTP flood). For each type, describe the specific defense mechanisms used, and explain why application-layer DDoS is the hardest to mitigate.
follow_up_shallow: How does rate limiting help against DDoS, and why is it not sufficient on its own?
follow_up_wrong: You said a firewall can block all DDoS traffic — volumetric attacks can saturate the network link before reaching the firewall. How is that addressed?
follow_up_strong: How does Cloudflare's Anycast-based DDoS mitigation distribute attack traffic across its global network, and what challenge does encrypted (HTTPS) attack traffic pose for inspection?

text: Design a zero-trust network architecture for a company transitioning from a traditional perimeter-based security model. Explain how authentication and authorization work at each layer, how micro-segmentation replaces traditional VLANs, and how you would secure traffic between microservices without relying on the network perimeter.
follow_up_shallow: What is the fundamental principle of zero trust that differs from traditional perimeter security?
follow_up_wrong: You said zero trust means no authentication is needed — it actually means the opposite: never trust, always verify. Can you reconsider?
follow_up_strong: How does Google's BeyondCorp implementation eliminate the distinction between internal and external networks, and what role does the access proxy play?

text: Explain how HTTP/3 with QUIC solves the head-of-line blocking problem that exists in HTTP/2 over TCP. Describe the specific mechanism TCP uses for multiplexing that causes the problem, how QUIC's use of UDP with independent streams solves it, and discuss the deployment challenges of QUIC in enterprise networks where UDP traffic is often blocked or rate-limited.
follow_up_shallow: What is head-of-line blocking at the TCP level, and why doesn't HTTP/2's stream multiplexing solve it?
follow_up_wrong: You said QUIC doesn't use any reliability mechanism — QUIC implements its own reliable delivery per-stream. How is this different from TCP's single byte stream?
follow_up_strong: How does QUIC implement connection migration so that a mobile client can switch from Wi-Fi to cellular without re-establishing the connection, and what identifier replaces the traditional 4-tuple?

text: Explain the difference between compile-time polymorphism and runtime polymorphism with concrete code examples. Why is method overloading resolved at compile time while method overriding is resolved at runtime?
follow_up_shallow: What mechanism does the compiler use to distinguish between overloaded methods — is it just the method name?
follow_up_wrong: You said overriding is resolved at compile time — if a base class reference points to a derived class object, how does the correct method get called?
follow_up_strong: Explain how vtables (virtual method tables) implement runtime polymorphism in C++, and what is the memory and performance overhead of virtual dispatch?

text: Explain the difference between an abstract class and an interface. In Java, when would you choose an abstract class over an interface, given that interfaces can now have default methods since Java 8?
follow_up_shallow: Can a class implement multiple interfaces? Can it extend multiple abstract classes? Why is there a difference?
follow_up_wrong: You said interfaces in Java can have constructors — they cannot. Why is this restriction in place?
follow_up_strong: How does the diamond problem manifest in languages with multiple inheritance versus Java's interface approach, and how does Java resolve conflicts when two interfaces provide default methods with the same signature?

text: Explain encapsulation beyond just 'hiding data.' How does encapsulation enable you to change the internal implementation of a class without breaking the code that uses it? Give a concrete example.
follow_up_shallow: What role do access modifiers (private, protected, public) play in achieving encapsulation?
follow_up_wrong: You said encapsulation means making all fields private — but what about the public methods? How do they relate to the encapsulated interface?
follow_up_strong: How does encapsulation relate to the Open/Closed Principle, and how do immutable objects provide stronger encapsulation guarantees?

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle? Give a concrete example where a subclass violates LSP and explain the real-world bug or design problem this violation causes.
follow_up_shallow: In the classic Rectangle-Square example, which class is the subclass, and why does making Square extend Rectangle violate LSP?
follow_up_wrong: You said LSP is about subclasses having the same methods — it's about behavioral compatibility, specifically preconditions and postconditions. Can you elaborate?
follow_up_strong: How does Design by Contract (preconditions, postconditions, invariants) formalize LSP, and how do languages like Eiffel enforce it compared to Java?

text: Explain the Single Responsibility Principle with a real-world example. If you have a class called UserService that handles user registration, email sending, and database persistence, how would you refactor it to follow SRP?
follow_up_shallow: How do you determine what constitutes a 'single responsibility' — is it one method, one feature, or one reason to change?
follow_up_wrong: You refactored by splitting into many tiny classes with one method each — SRP is about cohesion around a reason to change, not minimizing code per class. How would you group responsibilities?
follow_up_strong: How does SRP relate to the concept of cohesion, and what metrics (like LCOM) can quantitatively measure whether a class has too many responsibilities?

text: Explain the difference between composition and inheritance. Why do many experienced developers recommend 'favor composition over inheritance,' and give a concrete example where using inheritance leads to problems that composition solves?
follow_up_shallow: What is the 'fragile base class' problem, and how does composition avoid it?
follow_up_wrong: You said inheritance and composition are always interchangeable — there are 'is-a' relationships where inheritance is natural. Can you give such an example?
follow_up_strong: How do mixins and traits (as in Scala or Rust) provide an alternative to both traditional inheritance and composition?

text: Explain the Factory design pattern. In an application that needs to create different types of notification objects (Email, SMS, Push), how would you implement a Factory pattern, and what problem does it solve compared to using 'new' directly in the client code?
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between a Factory Method and an Abstract Factory?
follow_up_wrong: Your factory still uses if-else chains with 'new' — how would you use a factory registry or reflection to make it truly open for extension?
follow_up_strong: How does the Factory pattern relate to the Dependency Inversion Principle, and how would you combine it with Dependency Injection in a Spring or .NET application?

text: What is the Singleton design pattern? Implement a thread-safe Singleton in Java and explain why the naive implementation (lazy initialization without synchronization) fails in a multi-threaded environment.
follow_up_shallow: What is double-checked locking, and why is the volatile keyword necessary for it to work correctly in Java?
follow_up_wrong: Your implementation synchronizes the entire getInstance method — why is this a performance problem, and how does double-checked locking improve it?
follow_up_strong: Why do many developers consider Singleton an anti-pattern? How does it hinder testability, and what alternatives (like Dependency Injection) solve the same problem?

text: Explain the difference between coupling and cohesion. Why is 'high cohesion, low coupling' considered a desirable design goal? Give a specific example of a codebase where high coupling between modules caused a maintenance problem.
follow_up_shallow: What are the different types of coupling (content, common, control, stamp, data) from most to least harmful?
follow_up_wrong: You defined cohesion as having fewer methods — cohesion is about how related the responsibilities within a module are, not the count. Can you clarify?
follow_up_strong: How do microservices architectures enforce low coupling through API boundaries, and what happens when services share databases (the 'shared database' anti-pattern)?

text: What is Dependency Injection? Explain the three types of injection (constructor, setter, interface injection) and demonstrate how DI makes unit testing easier using a concrete example with a mock dependency.
follow_up_shallow: Why is constructor injection generally preferred over setter injection?
follow_up_wrong: You described DI as the Service Locator pattern — they solve similar problems but work differently. What's the key difference?
follow_up_strong: How do DI containers (like Spring's IoC container or .NET's built-in DI) manage object lifetimes (singleton, scoped, transient), and what problems arise from mismatched lifetimes?

text: Explain the Observer design pattern and describe a real-world scenario where it is used. How does the Observer pattern achieve loose coupling between the subject and its observers? What are the potential problems with the Observer pattern, such as memory leaks?
follow_up_shallow: How does the Observer pattern relate to the publish-subscribe model used in event-driven architectures?
follow_up_wrong: You said the observer must know the concrete type of the subject — actually, the subject is typically referenced through an interface. Why is this important?
follow_up_strong: How do reactive programming libraries like RxJava extend the Observer pattern with backpressure handling, and why is backpressure important for high-throughput systems?

text: Explain the Strategy design pattern with a concrete example. You have a payment processing system that needs to support credit card, PayPal, and cryptocurrency payments. Show how Strategy eliminates the need for conditional statements and makes the system open for extension.
follow_up_shallow: How does the Strategy pattern differ from the Template Method pattern, given that both allow varying behavior?
follow_up_wrong: Your implementation has the context class directly instantiating strategy objects — this recreates tight coupling. How would you use DI or a Factory to fix this?
follow_up_strong: How can you combine Strategy with the Registry pattern to allow runtime discovery and loading of new payment strategies without recompiling the application?

text: Explain the Open/Closed Principle with a concrete example. Show a class design that violates OCP, then refactor it to satisfy OCP. How does this principle relate to using polymorphism instead of if-else or switch statements?
follow_up_shallow: What does 'open for extension, closed for modification' specifically mean in terms of code changes when adding new functionality?
follow_up_wrong: You said OCP means you should never change existing code — actually, OCP means the behavior can be extended without modifying source code. How is this different?
follow_up_strong: How does the Expression Problem relate to OCP, and why is it difficult to satisfy OCP along both the data dimension and the operation dimension simultaneously?

text: Explain the Decorator design pattern. You are building a coffee shop ordering system where customers can add toppings (milk, sugar, whipped cream) to a base coffee. How would you use the Decorator pattern to handle pricing, and why is this better than creating a subclass for every combination?
follow_up_shallow: How does Decorator differ from inheritance in terms of combining behaviors?
follow_up_wrong: Your decorator doesn't implement the same interface as the component it wraps — this breaks the pattern. Why must the decorator have the same interface?
follow_up_strong: How does Java's I/O stream library (InputStream, BufferedInputStream, DataInputStream) use the Decorator pattern, and what are the practical downsides of deep decorator chains?

text: Explain the Interface Segregation Principle. A developer has created a single large interface called IWorker with methods work(), eat(), and sleep(). A Robot class implements IWorker but cannot eat() or sleep(). How does this violate ISP, and how would you refactor it?
follow_up_shallow: How many interfaces would you create from the original IWorker, and what methods would each contain?
follow_up_wrong: You said ISP means every interface should have only one method — it's about client-specific interfaces, not single-method interfaces. What's the distinction?
follow_up_strong: How does ISP interact with the Dependency Inversion Principle, and how do role interfaces in DDD (Domain-Driven Design) embody ISP?

text: Explain the Dependency Inversion Principle with a concrete example. Show how a high-level module (e.g., an OrderProcessor) that directly depends on a low-level module (e.g., MySQLDatabase) violates DIP, and how introducing an abstraction layer fixes it.
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between Dependency Injection and Dependency Inversion — are they the same thing?
follow_up_wrong: You said DIP means high-level modules should depend on low-level modules — it's the opposite. Both should depend on abstractions. Can you correct your design?
follow_up_strong: How does the Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters) pattern embody DIP at the architectural level, and how does it enable testing business logic without databases or frameworks?

text: Explain the Adapter design pattern. Your application uses a legacy PaymentGateway with a method processPayment(amount, currency) but your new system expects services implementing a ModernPayment interface with a method pay(paymentDetails). Design an adapter and explain the trade-offs between class adapter (using inheritance) and object adapter (using composition).
follow_up_shallow: In your implementation, which type of adapter (class or object) did you use, and why?
follow_up_wrong: You modified the legacy PaymentGateway class directly — the Adapter pattern's purpose is to work with existing code without modification. How would you wrap it instead?
follow_up_strong: How does the Adapter pattern relate to the Anti-Corruption Layer in Domain-Driven Design, and when should you build an ACL versus directly adapting?

text: Explain the different types of constructors in an object-oriented language: default, parameterized, copy, and move constructors. When does the compiler automatically generate each, and when must the programmer explicitly define them?
follow_up_shallow: What happens if you define a parameterized constructor but not a default constructor — can you still create an object with no arguments?
follow_up_wrong: You said the copy constructor always performs a deep copy — by default, the compiler-generated copy constructor does shallow copy. What problems can this cause?
follow_up_strong: Explain the Rule of Three/Five/Zero in C++ and how move semantics introduced in C++11 changed the guidelines for special member function management.

text: You need to design a plugin system for an IDE that allows third-party developers to add new language support, themes, and debugger integrations. Each plugin type has different capabilities, and plugins must be loadable at runtime without recompiling the IDE. Apply SOLID principles and appropriate design patterns to design this system. Justify each pattern choice and discuss how you would handle plugin versioning and backwards compatibility.
follow_up_shallow: Which SOLID principles are most critical for a plugin architecture, and why?
follow_up_wrong: Your design requires recompiling the main application when adding a new plugin type — how do you make it truly extensible?
follow_up_strong: How does Eclipse's OSGi-based plugin system handle dependency resolution and class loading isolation between plugins, and what problems does this solve?

text: You are reviewing a codebase where the developers have used inheritance hierarchies that are 6+ levels deep, God objects with 50+ methods, service locator for dependency resolution, and Singleton pattern for database connections. For each of these design decisions, explain the specific problems it causes, how it violates OOP principles, and provide a concrete refactoring strategy.
follow_up_shallow: Which of these four issues would you prioritize refactoring first, and why?
follow_up_wrong: You said deep inheritance is fine as long as each class adds functionality — what problems does it cause for maintenance and testing?
follow_up_strong: How does technical debt from poor OOP design compound over time, and what metrics (cyclomatic complexity, coupling between objects, depth of inheritance tree) would you track to prevent it?

text: Compare the Gang of Four design patterns with modern alternatives. For the Observer pattern, explain how reactive streams (RxJava, Project Reactor) improve upon the classic Observer. For the Strategy pattern, explain how first-class functions in languages like Python, JavaScript, or Kotlin can replace the traditional class-based Strategy. For the Singleton pattern, explain how DI containers provide a better solution. Are the original GoF patterns still relevant in modern software development?
follow_up_shallow: Which GoF patterns have become less relevant in functional programming languages, and which remain important?
follow_up_wrong: You said all GoF patterns are obsolete — many are still widely used. Can you identify which ones remain essential regardless of programming paradigm?
follow_up_strong: How do algebraic data types and pattern matching in languages like Rust and Scala replace the Visitor pattern, and why is this considered more type-safe?

text: Design an object model for a multi-tenant SaaS application where each tenant can have customized business rules, workflows, and data schemas. The core platform must remain a single codebase while supporting radically different tenant configurations. Apply OOP principles to design the extension points, and discuss how you balance flexibility with code complexity. Address the specific challenge of a tenant wanting to add a custom field to the User entity that doesn't exist in the base schema.
follow_up_shallow: How would you handle tenant-specific validation rules without modifying the core domain models?
follow_up_wrong: Your design requires a separate code branch for each tenant — this doesn't scale. How would you make customization data-driven instead?
follow_up_strong: How does Salesforce's metadata-driven architecture allow each tenant to have custom objects and fields without separate database schemas, and what are the performance implications of this approach?

text: Discuss the tension between OOP principles and performance optimization. In a high-frequency trading system processing millions of events per second, explain why deep inheritance hierarchies with virtual dispatch, heavy use of polymorphism through interfaces, and small immutable value objects can become performance bottlenecks. What OOP design compromises would you make, and how do techniques like data-oriented design provide alternatives?
follow_up_shallow: What is the cache miss penalty of accessing data through pointer indirection in OOP, and why does this matter?
follow_up_wrong: You said OOP is always too slow for high-performance systems — many high-performance systems use OOP. The key is understanding which OOP features have runtime cost. Which do?
follow_up_strong: How does Entity Component System (ECS) architecture used in game engines achieve cache-friendly data layout while still providing composability similar to OOP?

text: Explain the difference between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega notation. Why do software engineers predominantly use Big-O in interviews and everyday analysis rather than Big-Theta, even when Big-Theta would be more precise?
follow_up_shallow: Is O(n) the same as Θ(n)? Give an example of an algorithm where the Big-O upper bound and the Big-Theta tight bound differ.
follow_up_wrong: You said Big-O is the average case — Big-O is an upper bound on growth rate, not about cases. What's the relationship between Big-O and worst case?
follow_up_strong: Why is amortized analysis sometimes more appropriate than worst-case Big-O, and how does the banker's or physicist's method work for analyzing dynamic arrays?

text: Compare arrays and linked lists in terms of time complexity for insertion at the beginning, access by index, and deletion from the middle. For a system that needs frequent insertions at arbitrary positions but rare random access, which would you choose?
follow_up_shallow: What is the cache performance difference between arrays and linked lists, and why does this sometimes matter more than the Big-O analysis?
follow_up_wrong: You said linked list insertion is O(1) for any position — finding the position is O(n). The insertion itself is O(1) only if you already have a pointer. Can you clarify?
follow_up_strong: How do skip lists provide O(log n) expected search time while maintaining the insertion flexibility of linked lists?

text: Describe two real-world applications where a stack is the most appropriate data structure and two where a queue is more appropriate. For each application, explain why the other data structure (stack vs queue) would fail or be inefficient.
follow_up_shallow: How would you implement a queue using two stacks? What is the time complexity of each operation?
follow_up_wrong: You used a queue for undo/redo functionality — undo requires LIFO order. Why doesn't FIFO work here?
follow_up_strong: What is a monotonic stack, and how is it used to solve the 'next greater element' type of problems in O(n) time?

text: What property must a Binary Search Tree satisfy, and what is the worst-case time complexity for search in an unbalanced BST? Explain with a concrete example how inserting sorted data into a BST degrades its performance and how a self-balancing BST prevents this.
follow_up_shallow: What is the height of a balanced BST with n nodes, and what is the height of a worst-case unbalanced BST?
follow_up_wrong: You said a BST always has O(log n) search time — that's only for balanced BSTs. What is the worst case for an unbalanced BST?
follow_up_strong: Compare AVL trees and Red-Black trees in terms of balance strictness, rotation frequency, and which operations each optimizes. When would you choose one over the other?

text: Explain the heap data structure and the heap property. Why is a max-heap ideal for implementing a priority queue? Walk through the heapify process when inserting a new element that is larger than its parent.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of insert and extract-max operations in a binary max-heap?
follow_up_wrong: You said a heap is always a sorted array — a heap only guarantees the heap property (parent ≥ children), not a fully sorted order. Can you correct this?
follow_up_strong: Explain how building a heap from an unsorted array can be done in O(n) time, not O(n log n), using the bottom-up heapify approach. Why is this faster?

text: Compare adjacency list and adjacency matrix representations for graphs. For a social network with 1 million users where the average user has 200 friends, calculate the memory usage for each representation and explain which is more appropriate.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of checking if two specific nodes are connected in each representation?
follow_up_wrong: You calculated the adjacency matrix as needing 1 million bytes — an adjacency matrix for n nodes requires n² entries. What does that equal for 1 million users?
follow_up_strong: How do graph databases like Neo4j store graphs internally, and why is their representation different from both adjacency list and matrix?

text: Compare BFS and DFS in terms of their traversal order, the data structure each uses, and their space complexity. Give two specific problems where BFS is the correct choice and two where DFS is better, explaining why.
follow_up_shallow: Why does BFS guarantee finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph while DFS does not?
follow_up_wrong: You said DFS uses a queue — DFS uses a stack (or recursion). What data structure does BFS use?
follow_up_strong: What is iterative deepening DFS, and how does it combine the space efficiency of DFS with the shortest-path guarantee of BFS?

text: Explain hash tables and the concept of collision resolution. Compare chaining (using linked lists) and open addressing (linear probing) for handling collisions. What happens to hash table performance as the load factor increases?
follow_up_shallow: What is the load factor, and what is a typical threshold that triggers rehashing in Java's HashMap?
follow_up_wrong: You said hash tables always have O(1) operations — in the worst case with many collisions, what is the time complexity?
follow_up_strong: How does Robin Hood hashing improve upon linear probing, and what is cuckoo hashing's guarantee for worst-case lookup time?

text: Compare stable and unstable sorting algorithms. Name two stable and two unstable sorting algorithms, and give a concrete example where using a stable sort produces a different (and correct) result compared to an unstable sort.
follow_up_shallow: Is quicksort stable? What about merge sort? How does stability relate to in-place sorting?
follow_up_wrong: You said sorting stability doesn't matter in practice — consider sorting employees by name, then by department. Why does stability matter here?
follow_up_strong: Prove that any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case. What sorting algorithms break this bound, and how?

text: Explain what a Trie data structure is and why it is more efficient than a hash table for certain string operations. Give a specific use case where a Trie significantly outperforms a hash map.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of inserting and searching for a word in a Trie, and what does it depend on?
follow_up_wrong: You said Trie search is O(n) where n is the number of stored words — it's actually O(m) where m is the length of the search key. Why?
follow_up_strong: How does a compressed trie (radix tree/Patricia tree) reduce memory usage, and how is it used in IP routing tables?

text: Explain the two properties that a problem must have for dynamic programming to be applicable: overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure. Use the Fibonacci sequence to illustrate overlapping subproblems and show the difference between a naive recursive solution and a DP solution.
follow_up_shallow: What is the difference between top-down (memoization) and bottom-up (tabulation) approaches to DP?
follow_up_wrong: You said every recursive problem can be solved with DP — DP requires overlapping subproblems. Give an example of a recursive problem without overlapping subproblems.
follow_up_strong: How do you identify the state and transition in a DP problem? Walk through the thought process for the Longest Common Subsequence problem.

text: Explain the fundamental difference between a greedy algorithm and a dynamic programming algorithm for solving optimization problems. For the coin change problem, explain why a greedy approach works for certain coin denominations (like US coins) but fails for others, and when DP is necessary.
follow_up_shallow: What property must a problem have for a greedy algorithm to produce an optimal solution?
follow_up_wrong: You said greedy always works when elements are sorted — that's not sufficient. What is the 'greedy choice property' and how do you prove it?
follow_up_strong: How do you formally prove that a greedy algorithm is optimal using an exchange argument or matroid theory?

text: Compare quicksort and merge sort in terms of time complexity (best, average, worst), space complexity, stability, and cache performance. In what situations is quicksort preferred despite its O(n²) worst case?
follow_up_shallow: How does the choice of pivot affect quicksort's performance, and what pivot selection strategies mitigate worst-case behavior?
follow_up_wrong: You said merge sort is always faster than quicksort — in practice, quicksort often outperforms merge sort. Why?
follow_up_strong: How does Timsort (used in Python and Java) combine the best properties of merge sort and insertion sort, and what is its adaptive behavior on nearly-sorted data?

text: Explain how a balanced BST like a Red-Black tree maintains balance during insertions. What are the Red-Black tree properties, and walk through a specific insertion that requires rotations to maintain these properties?
follow_up_shallow: What are the five Red-Black tree properties, and which one does a new insertion potentially violate?
follow_up_wrong: You said Red-Black trees require the same number of rotations as AVL trees — Red-Black trees need fewer rotations per insertion. Why?
follow_up_strong: How do left-leaning Red-Black trees simplify the implementation, and what is the relationship between a 2-3-4 tree and a Red-Black tree?

text: Explain what a Segment Tree is and what class of problems it solves. Compare its time and space complexity with a naive approach for range sum queries with point updates on an array.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of build, query, and update operations for a Segment Tree?
follow_up_wrong: You said a Segment Tree query is O(n) — by exploiting the tree structure, queries can be answered in O(log n). How?
follow_up_strong: How does lazy propagation in Segment Trees allow range updates in O(log n) instead of O(n log n)?

text: Explain the difference between separate chaining and open addressing in hash tables. Design a hash table that needs to support 10 million entries with a maximum of 5% collision rate. How would you choose the hash function, table size, and load factor? What happens when the load factor threshold is exceeded?
follow_up_shallow: What is the clustering problem in linear probing, and how does double hashing mitigate it?
follow_up_wrong: You said the hash table should never be resized — resizing is essential. What is the typical strategy and its amortized cost?
follow_up_strong: How does consistent hashing solve the remapping problem when hash table buckets are distributed across multiple servers?

text: Explain topological sorting and why it only works on Directed Acyclic Graphs. Give two different algorithms that produce a topological ordering and compare their approaches.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of both Kahn's BFS-based algorithm and the DFS-based algorithm for topological sort?
follow_up_wrong: You said topological sort works on graphs with cycles — what happens when you apply topological sort to a cyclic graph, and how can this be used to detect cycles?
follow_up_strong: How is topological sorting used in build systems (like Make) and package managers (like npm) to determine execution order, and how are cyclic dependencies handled?

text: Compare Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm with Bellman-Ford. What is the fundamental limitation of Dijkstra's that Bellman-Ford overcomes, and what is the practical cost of this capability? Walk through an example graph where Dijkstra's produces an incorrect result but Bellman-Ford succeeds.
follow_up_shallow: What is the time complexity of each algorithm, and how does using a priority queue improve Dijkstra's?
follow_up_wrong: You said Dijkstra's works with negative weights — it doesn't. Can you construct a simple graph with a negative edge where Dijkstra's fails?
follow_up_strong: How does the A* algorithm improve upon Dijkstra's using heuristics, and what properties must the heuristic function satisfy for A* to be optimal?

text: Explain the concept of amortized analysis and why it gives a more accurate picture of an algorithm's performance than worst-case analysis. Use the dynamic array (like Java's ArrayList or Python's list) as an example: explain why appending is O(1) amortized despite individual append operations sometimes being O(n).
follow_up_shallow: What is the typical growth factor for a dynamic array, and why is doubling used instead of adding a fixed number of slots?
follow_up_wrong: You said amortized analysis is the same as average case — amortized analysis considers the worst case over a sequence of operations, not probabilistic averages. What's the key difference?
follow_up_strong: Explain the potential method of amortized analysis and apply it to analyze the performance of a splay tree's operations.

text: You need to design the data structures and algorithms for a real-time autocomplete system that serves 100,000 queries per second with sub-10ms latency. The dictionary contains 10 million words, and results should be ranked by popularity. Compare a Trie-based approach with an inverted index approach. For the Trie approach, discuss how you handle memory efficiency, fuzzy matching (typo tolerance), and how you would update popularity rankings without blocking queries.
follow_up_shallow: How would you store popularity scores in a Trie — at each node or only at leaf nodes?
follow_up_wrong: You proposed searching all 10 million words for each query — that gives O(n) per query. How does a Trie reduce this?
follow_up_strong: How does Google's autocomplete handle personalized suggestions and trending queries in real-time, and what data structures enable this?

text: Analyze the time complexity of hash table operations in adversarial conditions. An attacker knows the hash function used by your web application's hash map and sends requests with specifically crafted keys that all hash to the same bucket. Explain the attack, its performance impact, and discuss three different defense mechanisms: cryptographic hash functions, universal hashing, and hash-flooding protections used in modern language runtimes.
follow_up_shallow: If all n keys hash to the same bucket with chaining, what is the time complexity of a lookup?
follow_up_wrong: You said using a cryptographic hash function eliminates the attack — if the function is deterministic and known, the attacker can still precompute collisions. How does randomized hashing help?
follow_up_strong: How does Java 8's HashMap switch from linked list to red-black tree when a bucket exceeds a threshold, and why was this specific mitigation chosen?

text: Compare B-trees and LSM-trees (Log-Structured Merge Trees) as the underlying data structures for database storage engines. Explain how each handles writes, reads, and range queries, and why B-trees dominate in traditional RDBMS (like PostgreSQL) while LSM-trees are preferred in write-heavy systems (like Cassandra, RocksDB). Discuss the write amplification and read amplification trade-offs of each.
follow_up_shallow: What is write amplification, and why is it a critical metric for storage engines?
follow_up_wrong: You said LSM-trees are always faster than B-trees — for read-heavy workloads, B-trees can be significantly faster. Why?
follow_up_strong: How does the compaction process in LSM-trees work, and what strategies (leveled vs size-tiered compaction) balance space amplification vs write amplification?

text: Design the data structures and algorithms for a social network's news feed system. The feed must merge posts from friends, pages, and groups, rank them by relevance (not just chronology), and paginate efficiently for infinite scroll. Discuss the trade-offs between fan-out-on-write (precomputing feeds) and fan-out-on-read (computing feeds at query time), and explain what data structure each approach primarily relies on.
follow_up_shallow: For fan-out-on-write, what happens when a celebrity with 10 million followers makes a post?
follow_up_wrong: You proposed sorting all posts every time a user opens their feed — this is O(n log n) per request. How would you pre-compute or incrementally maintain the feed?
follow_up_strong: How does Facebook's hybrid approach handle the hot/cold user distinction, using fan-out-on-write for ordinary users and fan-out-on-read for celebrities?

text: Discuss the theoretical lower bounds of comparison-based sorting (Ω(n log n)) and explain why certain non-comparison-based sorting algorithms like counting sort, radix sort, and bucket sort can achieve O(n) time complexity without violating this bound. For a system that sorts 100 million 32-bit integers, compare radix sort versus quicksort in terms of actual performance considering cache behavior, constant factors, and practical hardware constraints.
follow_up_shallow: Why are counting sort and radix sort not considered comparison-based, and what assumptions do they make about the input?
follow_up_wrong: You said radix sort violates the Ω(n log n) lower bound — it doesn't violate it because the bound only applies to comparison-based sorting. How does radix sort avoid comparisons?
follow_up_strong: How does external sorting (used when data exceeds RAM) combine merge sort with I/O-efficient algorithms, and what is the I/O complexity model?
